8 min read
Just now

--

One of the world’s most respected AI researchers just admitted something that changes everything we thought we knew about what it means to be a software engineer in 2026
📚 Special Edition of the AI for Everyone series — Part 1: What Even Is AI? | Part 2: Getting Started | Part 3: AI for Email | Part 4: AI at Work
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Christian Velitchkov on UnsplashAndrej Karpathy — former Director of AI at Tesla, OpenAI founding member, and one of the most respected researchers in the field — said something recently that stopped a lot of people in their tracks.
Speaking at Sequoia Ascent 2026 and in the No Priors podcast, he made two admissions that are still reverberating across the engineering world:
“I said recently that I have never felt more behind as a programmer.”
And then, even more striking:
“I probably haven’t hand-typed a single line of code since December 2025.”
Let that sink in. One of the people who helped build modern AI — someone with nearly two decades of deep technical experience — feels behind. Not behind on some obscure research topic. Behind as a programmer. In his own field.
If that’s where Karpathy is, where does that leave the rest of us?
That’s the question worth sitting with. And the answer — once you dig into what he’s actually saying — is both more nuanced and more actionable than the headline suggests.

December 2025: The Month Everything Changed
Karpathy is specific about the timing. This wasn’t a gradual drift. There was a threshold moment.
“Around December 2025, I felt a step change: the generated chunks got larger, more coherent, and more reliable. I started trusting the agents with more of the work.”
Before December, AI coding tools were helpful but still required constant correction. You’d generate a function, catch three mistakes, fix them, generate the next piece. Useful, but you were still fundamentally in the driver’s seat line by line.
After December, something shifted. The agents could handle what Karpathy calls “macro actions” — not just filling in the next line, but taking on entire chunks of meaningful work:

• Implement this feature

• Refactor this entire subsystem

• Research this library and propose an approach

• Write tests, run them, and fix the failures

• Compare approaches and recommend one with reasoning
The unit of work changed. From typing lines of code to delegating outcomes.
This is why he feels behind — not because he got worse, but because the game changed around him while he was still learning the new rules. And he’s someone who saw it coming from the inside.

“AI Psychosis” — What Karpathy Is Actually Describing
In the No Priors podcast, Karpathy used a phrase that immediately went viral: “AI psychosis.”
He wasn’t describing something clinical. He was describing a very real psychological shift that happens when you work at the frontier of this technology every day — a kind of anxiety that sets in when you’re not using AI. He admitted he feels uneasy when his subscription tokens go unused. That the idle compute represents missed opportunity.
He told colleagues at OpenAI something even more pointed: “If we’re successful, we’re all out of a job.”
This wasn’t a doomsday prediction. It was the honest reaction of someone who has watched AI go from curiosity to tool to colleague — and is watching it edge toward something more. The discomfort isn’t panic. It’s the feeling of standing at a threshold and knowing it.
Most engineers haven’t reached that threshold yet. But the trajectory is clear, and the gap between those who feel it and those who don’t is closing faster than most people realize.

Software 3.0: The Framing That Actually Helps
Karpathy has a way of naming things that makes them easier to think about. His latest framing is no exception.
He describes the history of software in three phases:
Software 1.0: Humans write explicit code. Every instruction is specified by hand. The program does exactly what you tell it — no more, no less.
Software 2.0: Humans create datasets, objectives, and neural networks. The program is learned from data rather than written by hand. Think recommendation algorithms, image classifiers, self-driving models.
Software 3.0: Humans program LLMs through prompts, context, tools, examples, memory, and instructions. The “program” is increasingly a set of natural language directions interpreted by an AI agent.
In Software 3.0, the context window — the instructions and information you give the AI — becomes the new source code. Writing good context is the new programming skill.
This is not just a technical reframing. It’s a career reframing. The skills that made someone excellent at Software 1.0 — precision typing, syntax memorization, knowing every API — matter less. The skills that make someone excellent at Software 3.0 — clear thinking, system design, knowing how to specify and verify outcomes — matter more.

The Skill That’s Rising: Agentic Engineering
Karpathy makes an important distinction that’s worth understanding carefully.
He separates vibe coding from agentic engineering.
Vibe coding — a term he coined — is using AI to build things by describing what you want, without worrying much about the underlying implementation. It raises the floor. Almost anyone can create something with it. It’s excellent for prototypes, personal projects, and quick experiments.
Agentic engineering is different. It’s the professional discipline of coordinating AI agents while preserving correctness, security, quality, and maintainability. It raises the ceiling. It’s what serious engineering teams need.
The agentic engineer doesn’t blindly accept generated code. They:

• Design clear specifications before delegating to agents

• Supervise plans and inspect what agents propose

• Review diffs with genuine critical judgment

• Write tests and evaluation loops to catch what agents miss

• Manage permissions and isolate what agents can touch

• Preserve the underlying system design the agent can’t fully understand
Karpathy gives a real example from his own work. An agent built a payment feature that matched Stripe purchases to Google accounts using email addresses. Plausible code. Bad system design — because the Stripe email and Google login email can be different users. A human needs enough product and engineering judgment to catch that and insist on persistent user IDs instead.
The agent wrote code. The engineer still had to understand the system.
“The frontier skill is not memorizing every API detail. Agents can remember whether a tensor library uses dim, axis, keepdim, reshape, or permute. The human still needs to understand the underlying concepts: storage, views, memory copies, invariants, identity, security boundaries, and the shape of the system.”
This is the most important thing Karpathy is saying — and it’s easy to miss in the noise about who typed what. Deep understanding matters more, not less, when agents are doing the typing.

The Verifiability Principle — Why Some Work Gets Automated First
If you want to understand which parts of engineering are changing fastest, Karpathy has a clean framework for that too.
“Traditional software automates what you can specify. LLMs and reinforcement learning automate what you can verify.”
If a task has an automatic success signal — tests pass or fail, programs run or crash, benchmarks go up or down — AI can practice it, get feedback, and improve rapidly. Coding is full of verifiable feedback loops, which is why coding agents feel dramatically more capable than agents in other domains.
This also means the domains where AI moves slowest are the ones with the least automatic feedback — nuanced human judgment, creative direction, relationship-based decisions, ethical reasoning. These are the domains where human expertise remains most durable.
For your own career, this framework is useful: if your work is highly verifiable and well-specified, AI is probably already encroaching on it. If your work requires judgment, context, relationships, and domain knowledge that resists easy measurement, you have more time — but not infinite time.

What This Means for Engineers Right Now
Karpathy is not describing a future. He’s describing what already changed, in his own workflow, at the end of last year. The question isn’t whether this shift is coming — it’s whether you’re adjusting to it.
Here’s what the adjustment actually looks like in practice:
Move from writing to directing. The primary skill is increasingly decomposing work into clear specifications that agents can execute, then reviewing and validating their output. Writing the code yourself, line by line, is becoming the slow path.
Invest in system understanding, not syntax. Agents remember APIs better than humans ever will. What they don’t understand is your system — its history, its constraints, its design decisions, its security boundaries. That’s what you need to protect and deepen.
Build evaluation habits. The engineers getting the most value from agents are the ones who’ve built reliable ways to check agent output — test suites, benchmarks, review processes. Agents produce volume; humans ensure quality.
Learn what “good” looks like fast. The most dangerous failure mode right now is shipping AI-generated code you don’t fully understand. Karpathy’s point isn’t that understanding matters less — it’s that you need to understand the right things, faster.
Embrace the discomfort. Karpathy — with two decades of experience and one of the sharpest technical minds in the field — says he feels behind. That feeling isn’t a sign you’re failing. It’s a sign you’re paying attention. Use it as fuel rather than a reason to freeze.

The Bigger Picture: What’s Actually Being Redefined
The reason Karpathy’s words landed so hard — and spread so fast — is that he’s articulating something a lot of engineers are feeling but haven’t put words to.
The identity of “software engineer” is being redefined. Not erased. Redefined.
For a long time, that identity was built around a specific set of technical skills — languages, frameworks, algorithms, systems. Those skills mattered because they were rare and hard to acquire. Producing working software required them.
The production of working software is becoming easier. Agents can generate it, debug it, test it, and iterate on it at a pace no human can match line by line.
What’s becoming more valuable — not less — is the judgment that sits above the code. The ability to understand a problem deeply enough to specify it clearly. The experience to know when a technically correct solution is architecturally wrong. The taste to know what should be built and what should be deleted. The integrity to review AI output honestly rather than ship whatever it generates.
Those are deeply human skills. And if Karpathy is right about where this is heading, they’re about to become the most important engineering skills of the decade.

The Bottom Line
Karpathy hasn’t typed a line of code since December. He feels more behind than ever. He’s worried about AI psychosis. And he’s one of the most thoughtful people working in this field.
None of that is cause for panic. It’s cause for honesty — with yourself, about where things actually stand and what you need to build.
The engineers who thrive in what’s coming won’t be the ones who typed the most code. They’ll be the ones who understood their systems deeply enough to direct agents well, reviewed agent output honestly enough to catch what it missed, and had the judgment to know the difference between software that works and software that’s good.
The threshold Karpathy crossed in December is moving toward all of us. The question is whether we meet it ready.

Read Karpathy’s Sequoia Ascent 2026 post in full at karpathy.bearblog.dev — it’s one of the clearest things written about where software engineering is heading.
